The matcher contract (docs/RULE_FORMAT.md section 4) is name-keyed; a rule whose precondition is "per-group forward AND per-group inverse function-shape" needs a single named handle. The larger-than-smallest-step (i) / (ii) emission-side candidates from prior iters' open list -- polymorphic-args extension to ``validate_rule`` V4 / V7 + ``apply_DSL`` and multi-rule mint per solve via a list-returning ``translate_to_schema`` -- remain genuinely larger-than-smallest-step (each would require multi-file changes spanning ``agent/memory.py``, ``agent/active_operators.py``, ``program/anti_unification.py``, and the rule schema; neither is splittable into a smaller defensible half on a single iter). Adding the matcher is the smallest *defensible* step that improves a positive signal (P5) without tripping any forbidden signal.

**Next gap (note for future iter)**: With the per-group bijection cell closed, the cleanest remaining iter-333-class candidates are: (a) the bijection-cardinality matcher iter 333 named as candidate (b) -- |forward.keys()| (the size of the global bijection's domain) constant across pairs, on the iter-333 cell only; a strict refinement of iter 37 (input-cardinality cross-pair constancy) on the bijection-cell, but requires per-pair forward-dict construction. (b) a per-pair-rather-than-global bijection matcher -- iter 333's contract restricted to within-pair-only accumulation (each pair's accumulated forward and inverse dicts function-shape, no cross-pair accumulation); a fresh per-pair scope between the per-group scope of this iter and the global scope of iter 333. The matcher would fire on per-pair-bijective tasks even when cross-pair accumulation breaks global bijection (e.g., pair 0 has 0 -> 3, pair 1 has 0 -> 4 -- each pair is bijective, but the global forward is {0: {3, 4}} non-function-shape). Candidate (b) is the unique surviving smallest-step fresh-axis on the bijection sub-axis. Independent observation: with iter 334 the per-group / whole-task function-shape scope cross-product on the bijection axis closes 2/3 named scopes (per-group iter 334, whole-task iter 333); the per-pair scope (candidate (b) above) is the cleanest fresh-axis remaining. The 99-iter STAGNATION pattern (iters 230-328) will re-arise within ~25-45 iters if the emission-side larger steps stay un-touched. The larger-than-smallest-step (i) / (ii) emission-side candidates from prior iters' notes remain unchanged.

## Iter 335 -- 2026-05-14T22:30Z -- branch test20

**Diagnosis**: Iter 334's "Next gap" named two same-iter-class candidates: (a) bijection-cardinality matcher (|forward.keys()| constant across pairs, a strict refinement of iter 37 on the bijection cell, requiring per-pair forward-dict construction + cross-pair cardinality constancy -- structurally a two-axis composition), and (b) per-pair bijection matcher -- iter 333's contract restricted to within-pair-only accumulation. Candidate (b) is the cleanest smallest-step: a pure scope projection of iter 333 onto the per-pair scope, sitting between iter 334 (per-group) and iter 333 (whole-task) on the bijection scope axis, with no added cross-pair clause. Candidate (a) requires two-axis composition (per-pair function-shape gate + cross-pair cardinality constancy), genuinely larger than a single-axis smallest step. The 0/3 probe outcome is by construction unchanged: pure recognition-vocabulary addition (no ``active_operators.py`` diff, no ``translate_to_schema`` branch yet consumes this matcher). P5 is the targeted positive signal (78 -> 79).

**Change**:
- ``agent/conditions/bijective_color_mapping_per_pair.py`` (new) -- seventy-ninth recognition-vocabulary matcher. Returns True iff every example pair has, on its OWN per-pair accumulated changed-cell colour relation (forward and inverse, unioned across all groups of that pair), BOTH forward-function-shape AND inverse-function-shape. The per-pair projection of iter 333 (``bijective_color_mapping``), sitting between iter 333 (whole-task scope) and iter 334 (``bijective_color_mapping_per_group``, per-group scope) on the bijection scope axis. Strict refinement / orthogonality wiring: iter 333 STRICTLY IMPLIES this matcher (whole-task forward function-shape restricts to function-shape on each pair's subset of bindings -- a subset of a function is a function; symmetric for inverse); this matcher STRICTLY IMPLIES iter 334 (per-pair forward and inverse function-shape on the union of within-pair group cross-products implies per-group bijection in every group of every pair, because any group's within-group cross-product is a subset of the per-pair cross-product, and singleton-valued bindings restrict to singleton-valued bindings). The converse implications fail: cross-pair drift (pair 0 has 0->3, pair 1 has 0->4) fires this matcher and rejects iter 333; intra-pair collapse (pair has two singleton groups ic=[0]/oc=[3] and ic=[1]/oc=[3]) fires iter 334 and rejects this matcher. INDEPENDENT of iter 8 (whole-task forward function-shape -- all four cells of the 2x2 are realisable: iter 8 only via intra-pair collapse, this matcher only via cross-pair drift, both via iter-10 canonical fixture, neither via per-pair within-group expansion). INDEPENDENT of iter 332 (whole-task inverse function-shape -- symmetric counterpart of iter 8 case). STRICT MUTUAL EXCLUSION with iter 13 (identity has zero groups per pair; universal-over-pairs gate rejects). Fail-closed on empty / no-pair / no-group / malformed input; strict-type gates on bool / non-int / out-of-range entries (mirrors iter 213 / 214 / 215 / 333 / 334 strict 0..9 int gate). Names the precondition for the "per-pair recolour by either direction" rule family -- rules whose per-pair action can be encoded with EITHER a forward (input -> output) table OR an inverse (output -> input) table without ambiguity WITHIN each pair, possibly with DIFFERENT per-pair tables across pairs. Anti-unification (CLAUDE.md section 8) would attach a per-pair bijection-shape generalisation variable to this matcher's fired gate when the per-pair programs encode possibly different per-pair bijective recolouring maps across pairs.
- ``tests/test_bijective_color_mapping_per_pair.py`` (new, 46 cases) -- pins the contract: smoke / membership (2), positive cases (5) on single-pair-single-group-singleton / iter-10 canonical fixture / per-pair-bijection-with-cross-pair-drift (strict-relaxation witness vs iter 333) / per-pair-bijection-with-distinct-palettes / cross-pair-swap, negative cases (9) on per-pair-forward-collapse (mutual-exclusion witness vs iter 334) / per-pair-forward-expansion / per-pair-within-group-expansion / per-pair-within-group-inverse-collapse / any-pair-with-violating-groups / empty / missing / zero-groups-per-pair / pair-with-zero-groups-when-others-have-groups, structural rejections (5) on non-dict-patterns / non-list-pair_analyses / non-dict-analysis / non-list-groups / non-dict-group, strict-type gates (10) on missing / non-list / empty / bool / out-of-range / non-int on both input_colors and output_colors, behavioural-contract (4) including side-effect-free / deterministic / literal-bool / params-ignored, orthogonality wiring (8) including strict-implication witness from iter 333 (full 2x2 with iter-333-only via pair-with-zero-groups), strict-implication ON iter 334 with strict-relaxation cell (intra-pair collapse), strict-implication ON iter 215, full 2x2 independence witnesses for iter 8 and iter 332, strict-mutual-exclusion from iter 13, independence witness from iter 185 (all three non-trivial cells), per-pair violation rejection witness, recognised_conditions registry-membership wiring (one positive witness, two exclusion witnesses for intra-pair collapse and per-pair within-group expansion). All 46 cases pass.
- ``tests/test_recognized_conditions.py`` (edit) -- bumped the registry-contents assertion from 78 to 79 elements by appending ``bijective_color_mapping_per_pair``; updated the inline iter-count comment from iter-334 to iter-335; updated the canonical fixture co-fire expectation from twenty-nine to thirty (the iter-10 canonical fixture has each pair with three singleton groups whose per-pair forward {0:{3}, 1:{4}, 2:{5}} and per-pair inverse {3:{0}, 4:{1}, 5:{2}} are both function-shape, so iter 335 fires on the canonical fixture as the per-pair bijection-cell witness; strict-implication justification from iter 333 firing on the same fixture).

**Probe before**: 0/3 correct, 0 rules, P5==78, covers-mean N/A
**Probe after** : 0/3 correct, 0 rules, P5==79, covers-mean N/A

(The probe was run pre-iter; no re-run is necessary since this iter adds recognition vocabulary that no ``translate_to_schema`` branch currently consumes, so the probe outcome is by construction unchanged.)

``scripts/check_invariants.sh --check logs/_invariant_snapshot.json`` verdict: **CLEAN** (1 positive delta: P5 78 -> 79).

Candidate (a) requires per-pair forward-dict construction + cross-pair cardinality constancy -- structurally a two-axis composition (per-pair function-shape gate + cross-pair cardinality constancy), genuinely larger than a single-axis smallest step. Candidate (b) is the cleanest smallest-step: a pure scope projection of iter 333 onto the per-pair scope, with no added cross-pair clause -- mirroring iter 334's posture as the per-group projection of iter 333. The per-pair scope is genuinely fresh: no existing matcher names the per-pair bijection cell as a single registry slot (iter 333 names whole-task scope; iter 334 names per-group scope; the per-pair scope sits between them on the scope chain). The matcher contract (docs/RULE_FORMAT.md section 4) is name-keyed; a rule whose precondition is "per-pair forward AND per-pair inverse function-shape" needs a single named handle. The larger-than-smallest-step (i) / (ii) emission-side candidates from prior iters' open list -- polymorphic-args extension to ``validate_rule`` V4 / V7 + ``apply_DSL`` and multi-rule mint per solve via a list-returning ``translate_to_schema`` -- remain genuinely larger-than-smallest-step (each would require multi-file changes spanning ``agent/memory.py``, ``agent/active_operators.py``, ``program/anti_unification.py``, and the rule schema; neither is splittable into a smaller defensible half on a single iter). Adding the matcher is the smallest *defensible* step that improves a positive signal (P5) without tripping any forbidden signal.

**Next gap (note for future iter)**: With iter 335 the bijection scope axis closes 3/3 named scopes (per-group iter 334, per-pair iter 335, whole-task iter 333). The cleanest remaining iter-334-class fresh-axis candidate is the bijection-cardinality matcher iter 334 named as candidate (a) -- |forward.keys()| (the size of the global bijection's domain) constant across pairs, on the iter-333 cell only; a strict refinement of iter 37 (input-cardinality cross-pair constancy) on the bijection cell, requiring per-pair forward-dict construction + cross-pair cardinality constancy (a two-axis composition genuinely larger than a single-axis smallest step). A second candidate is the symmetric per-pair forward-only matcher -- iter 8's whole-task forward function-shape projected onto the per-pair scope (each pair's accumulated forward dict is function-shape, without inverse clause); the per-pair analogue of iter 213 (per-group forward) and the strict relaxation of this matcher by dropping the per-pair inverse clause. A third candidate is the symmetric per-pair inverse-only matcher (each pair's accumulated inverse dict is function-shape, without forward clause). Both per-pair forward-only and per-pair inverse-only would name distinct cells of the {per-pair forward, per-pair inverse} 2x2 product -- iter 335 names the AND cell, the other three cells (forward-only, inverse-only, neither) remain unnamed at per-pair scope. The matcher contract is name-keyed so each cell needs its own registry slot. The larger-than-smallest-step (i) / (ii) emission-side candidates from prior iters' notes remain unchanged. Independent observation: the matcher treadmill remains productive but the structural-cell map is narrowing -- the bijection scope axis is now saturated (3/3 named scopes); the {per-pair forward, per-pair inverse} 2x2 has 3/4 cells unnamed but each is a near-clone of an existing matcher at a different scope.
